notes: Official Treasury product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
In Seattle, your rent of `$2,350` sits right in the "missing middle" where specific programs and negotiation tactics can yield significant savings. 

### 1. The MFTE & MHA Program Check
Seattle has two primary programs that offer rent-restricted units in market-rate buildings. At `$2,350/month`, you may be paying a full market rate while an identical unit in your building (or the one next door) is priced at a restricted rate.

*   **MFTE (Multifamily Tax Exemption):** Targets households earning roughly **60–90% of Area Median Income (AMI)**. For a single renter in Seattle, this often covers incomes up to ~$80k–$95k depending on the specific building's program version.
*   **MHA (Mandatory Housing Affordability):** Provides more deeply affordable units, typically capped at **60% AMI** for units larger than 400 sq ft.

**The Strategy:** 
Before renewing, check the [Seattle Office of Housing MFTE Participant Map](https://www.seattle.gov/housing/renters/find-housing). If your current building participates, you can ask the leasing office if any MFTE units are available for transfer. If not, look for "P7" (Program 7) buildings nearby—these new buildings have strict rent escalation caps (often the lesser of AMI growth or 4.5%).

> **Eligibility Warning:** Income is verified only at move-in. If you qualify now and your income increases later, you generally keep the lower rent. However, you must include all gross income: base salary, **Microsoft RSU vests**, and bonuses.

| Program | Target AMI | Est. Rent for 1BR (Approx) | Potential Monthly Savings |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Market Rate** | N/A | `$2,350` (Current) | `$0` |
| **MFTE (75% AMI)** | ~$82k | `~$1,950` | **`$400`** |
| **MFTE (65% AMI)** | ~$71k | `~$1,700` | **`$650`** |

---

### 2. Lease Renewal Negotiation
Landlords in Seattle typically face **`$1,000–$5,000`** in turnover costs (vacancy, cleaning, listing). You can use this as leverage, especially if you are a reliable tenant.

**The "Known Quantity" Tactic:**
If your landlord proposes an increase, counter by highlighting your track record.
*   **Check Comparable Units:** Look at Zillow/Redfin for your building. If the landlord is listing new units for `$2,200` with "6 weeks free," but asking you for `$2,450`, use that data.
*   **The Math:** A one-month "concession" (free month) on a `$2,350` lease is effectively a **`$195/month` discount**. Landlords often prefer giving a one-time "concession" month over lowering the base rent because it keeps the building's "on-paper" value higher.

---

### 3. Payment Strategy: Bilt Rewards
If you can't lower the rent, you can recover value through how you pay. Seattle is a high-adoption market for **Bilt Rewards**, which allows you to earn points on rent without the typical 2.9% credit card fee.

*   **Bilt Blue ($0 Fee):** Earns **1x points** on rent.
*   **Estimated Value:** On `$2,350/month`, you earn **28,200 points per year**. 
*   **Redemption:** These points transfer 1:1 to partners like **Alaska Airlines** (huge in Seattle) or **Hyatt**. At a conservative 2 cents per point value, that’s **`~$564/year`** in travel back in your pocket.

---

### Your Renewal Checklist
- [ ] **Run your MFTE math:** Add your base salary + last year's bonus + scheduled RSU vests for the next 12 months. Compare this to the [Seattle 2026 AMI Tables](https://www.seattle.gov/housing/property-managers/income-and-rent-limits).
- [ ] **Audit your building:** Search the [Office of Housing map](https://www.seattle.gov/housing/renters/find-housing) to see if your current building or immediate neighbors are MFTE/MHA participants.
- [ ] **Screenshot the "New Tenant" deals:** If your building is offering move-in specials to new residents, save those to use as leverage during your renewal conversation.
- [ ] **Request a 14-month lease:** Sometimes landlords will lower the monthly rate if you agree to a lease term that ends during a "peak" leasing month (like June-August) rather than winter.
